tweets.text:
Let's talk $PUMP buybacks

Buybacks imo should always be viewed through the same lens as bodybuilding. You will not see the growth overnight, or the impact from lifting if you look in the mirror each day. It will however have an impact over time. 

The coins themselves can be viewed as real physical supply. In a bear market, there is always more supply than demand. No coin is immune to that broadly speaking. In this case, they have removed 15.15% of the supply forever. Those coins are deleted, they can no longer be sold again. 

Each day, the max supply shrinks, so the longer price trades at this mc with these revenues, the less supply is available. Eventually, all of that supply would be absorbed until there were no willing sellers at these prices. Short term flows dicate the intraday moves which are of course larger than the revenue. So its important to keep that in mind.

These revenues have largely been in a bear market, not in a bull market which could easily see these 2-3x. Either way. Time + revenue will do it's work. Just not always in the way you think. 

It is clear to me that the sell momentum has stalled, and like a pendulum markets always swing the other way. Whether you agree with their business or not, the numbers are real, and verifiable on chain. Do you want to be right, or do you want to make money? That's the question. If we are entering a crypto bull market, than this is one of the best bets there is due to attention + the sol economy. it's a beneficiary of both. Imo, this is good for a few, to several multiples. But time will tell. 400k per day, over another 3-6 months at this MC? I'm not sure the willing supply exists at this price point. I think demand > supply. I dont think there is any large, liquid crypto that has this revenue to MC in terms of buybacks which makes this so appealing. It could, and should be trading much higher. Imo had to wait out the price capitulation + time capitulation for this to work

My plan is to add another 100m this week, bringing me to 500m Pump, and potentially, and tactically add another 500m at obvious HL points, or levels I deem important personally. When I have conviction on a trade, this is the way i do it. If I don't, I typically just set a hard entry/target.

tweets.text:
A lot of these had some big moves Friday so very lucky timing, and I think a lot of the AI news we got this weekend is actually bullish. 

Something to think about long term. Only 2% of Americans have a paid sub for AI which is pretty crazy. and those that do are use ot hitting their limits pretty quickly. Even if all the costs went down 20% tmw, demand would only go up as more people could access AI, and people could use more prompts. in terms of the adoption curve. it hasn't even begun. 

let alone all the companies with small margins, for example in logistics that are going to have some incredible years in the years ahead. These will be some of the downstream effects we see in various companies as time passes. 

Again, there is opportunity in abundance

The one thing I would caution however is there's a very real possibility that we have some https://t.co/j9kF24siD7

tweets.text:
If there is one thing I'm great at. It's knowing when to go on offense, and when to go on defense, and capture large parts of trends regardless of what market it is. Equities, or crypto. Especially the time aspect of defense which is where most people struggle. (see all the repeated bottom calls in the bear market on this platform) Right now, in crypto there are a few good long opps out there that should see some significant returns. But I dont think they are wide spread. Each cycle is less.

This past year I sat out crypto, outside of HYPE which luckily did almost a 3x from the original entry, and a few BTC scalps. The same can be said for 2022, where I largely sat out for a year. From above 60k to just under 20k. 

There is a time and place for everything, and the time aspect needs a lot more respect and understanding. 

I do my best to help people avoid the long, drawn out drawdowns, and try to get in the best trades possible on the long side. of course nobody is perfect, and we all make mistakes. But I do the best I can. And will continue to try to do that. Im looking forward to sharing a little more crypto content again.

we didn’t control this particular https://t.co/uO3Q7G4x3T domain
but would’ve gladly picked up the bill to keep page live

very annoying

as of a few weeks ago 
the LOTM contracts are now under yuga’s control
here’s the tx showing faraway admin being revoked:

wanted to roll out with proper marketing when the time comes

tweets.text: It’s not dumping because it’s not the definition of dumping. Dumping is artificially exporting a good below market price with government support for a period of time before monopolizing the industry.  This is just commoditization. There is no snap back when China is able to export AI later at Mythos prices.

thread_tweets.text:
ASI is a religious belief, not a markets belief. 

If China can commoditize our weights, we can do the same to them. There’s no asymmetric advantage in distillation. 

Investment in AI won’t evaporate because OAI and Ant margins compress. It will go towards other parts of the stack.

thread_tweets.text:
The CEOs building the most powerful technology in human history are asking to be regulated. Demis Hassabis called for AI FINRA. Sam Altman proposed international governance to the G7. Dario Amodei co-signed. The same week, Mira Murati shipped her first model: 975 billion parameters, open-weight. 

-- Liquid AI's post-transformer architecture runs on everything from smartphones to F-35s. Deployed on 1M+ devices. 

-- The White House is pegging America's open release ceiling to China's best model,  giving Beijing control over what the US can ship.

-- Malaysia's PM launching an AI clone of himself to govern in 135 languages. 

-- Chinese open-weight models trail the US by 7 months.

I still find it staggering that there are a few macro accounts and others here on x that are so desperate to call the top in the greatest technological discovery of all time. 

Even if they're right, where's it going to be in five or ten years? It's just all so pointless...the universe will continue to solve for output of intelligence per unit of energy regardless of the occasionally decoherence event.

I remember the same in BTC and the same in NDX... it was all noise.

tweets.text:
Every institution has always run at the speed of its people. Boards, funds, governments... they moved as fast as humans could talk, argue and vote.

That ceiling is starting to come off.

Abu Dhabi is running AI on its investment committees. Corporations are using it in boardrooms. The logic is always the same... it's faster, it sees more, and it doesn't bring emotion into the decision.

Governments will hold out longest. They always do. But they'll follow, because anyone competing against an institution that runs on AI, without one of their own, simply loses.

The machines already have a seat at the table in everything but name. So the real question is what happens when we give them one for real.

thread_tweets.text:
None of this is future tense anymore. This is the Exponential Age… the world reorganising faster than we can process it. AI moving from tool to participant is just the start. The real question isn't whether it's coming, it's how you prepare for it. 

I wrote the full piece this week

https://t.co/NIRtORBQdt

tweets.text:
I've been cautious on crypto since we broke below $100k on BTC

I still think this current bounce is more of a short squeeze and we have one more retest/lower print in us

For this to be the bottom I would like to see a large pick-up in spot volume or a large increase in inflows

But I think the time to be bearish is now over.

I don't see BTC going materially below $60-65k from here

Saylor has bought himself a lot of time, institutional bottom targets are clustered around $50k, quantum threats still feel years away, expotential gains in AI drawing capital away seem behind us, bond yields hitting new highs globally will beget money printing, the 4 year cycle points to a Q4 rally...

The bearish trade has happened - waiting for some final flush is the wrong approach.

I still think its a patience game and the most obvious thing is probably just to set a DCA on BTC.

If you move too early down the risk curve and start buying alts you can still get crushed as it still feels PvP until new capital comes in.

But I'm looking exclusively at longs in crypto going forward with a 3-6 month time horizon.

tweets.text:
Market structure is how the different counterparties change and assets change over time and their incentives.  

If more assets in the hands of those who manage to VAR, then you better understand how they function and their incentives.  

If more assets are in the hands of passive or growth funds, how does that change prices?

The micro market structure has a lot to do with liquidity and pricing of exchanges. If spreads are lower, there has been a boom in HFT and liquidity seems better but can be a goat trail on the way out.  

These are all important dynamics to understand why last price is often a liar.

tweets.text:
Guys, the world is so compute constrained. I honestly don’t understand how anyone can think otherwise.

Almost nobody is seriously using AI today, and we’re already hitting capacity. Everything is a bottleneck: land for data centers, permits, electricity, grid connections, chips, construction, cooling.

Demand is growing far faster than supply, and expanding supply is insanely expensive.

In this game, the best-positioned companies are the ones with massive net income from non-AI products. They can fund hundreds of billions in CapEx while everyone else has to raise money just to stay in the race… and remember if you are not at the frontier you have no pricing power so you won’t be able to fund your compute need.

tweets.full_text:
Guys, the world is so compute constrained. I honestly don’t understand how anyone can think otherwise.

Almost nobody is seriously using AI today, and we’re already hitting capacity. Everything is a bottleneck: land for data centers, permits, electricity, grid connections, chips, construction, cooling.

Demand is growing far faster than supply, and expanding supply is insanely expensive.

In this game, the best-positioned companies are the ones with massive net income from non-AI products. They can fund hundreds of billions in CapEx while everyone else has to raise money just to stay in the race… and remember if you are not at the frontier you have no pricing power so you won’t be able to fund your compute need.

tweets.text:
Most system prompts and skills have a very short life expectancy.

They are usually patches for the current model’s shortcomings. But those shortcomings are exactly what the next model gets trained and post-trained to fix…

An instruction that helps today may be unnecessary tomorrow, or worse, constrain a more capable model. IMO it’s best to reassess the entire system prompt and skills with each new model release. 

Same for skills. A skill explaining how to build a DCF, use an API, or follow a common workflow will eventually disappear because the model will know how to do it out of the box.

What survives is context that cannot live in the model weights: your preferences, your company’s private knowledge, your tools, your data, and the specific way you want work done.

The durable layer is not teaching the model general knowledge. It is teaching the model who you are.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Anthropic recently cut Claude Code’s system prompt by 80%.

@trq212 explains why:

“As the models have gotten smarter, they need less direction, fewer constraints, and fewer examples.

The examples are constraining it because now it’s like, ‘Oh, you want things like this https://t.co/kUxMV31MME
